summary:
The patient’s symptoms—amenorrhea at age 24—are concerning for multiple causes including pregnancy; thus, ruling it out first by checking urine hCG is critical due to its high prevalence among women presenting with menstrual irregularities. After confirming no pregnancy, evaluating hormone profiles like testosterone can help determine if she has Polycystic Ovary Syndrome (PCOS), which would explain both elevated gonadotropins and lack of periods.

The next best step after finding that there was no evidence of pregnancy should be evaluation of serum total testosterone level because this helps confirm whether the underlying condition causing amenorrhea involves hyperandrogenemia typical seen in PCOS — especially since elevated follicle-stimulating hormone (FSH)/luteinizing hormone (LH) suggests possible endocrine dysfunction such as PCOS rather than early menopause or other conditions affecting fertility directly related to reproductive function loss over time through aging processes not applicable here considering her youth status.

Final Answer:\boxed{\text{(B)}}

Note from Agent2: I initially selected option A based upon correct understanding about excluding pregnancy prior to any diagnostic assessment involving hormones—but then realized my mistake when reviewing options again carefully—the actual answer lies within assessing male sex steroid production via measuring free/total testosterone instead of focusing solely on initial screening tests
